content:
# Exodus Chapter 13 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~39r–39v (PDF pp. 75–76); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 53–54 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_013_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_013_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_013_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_013_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## Consecration of the Firstborn (vv. 1–2)

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Consecrate [**qaddesh**] to Me every firstborn [**bekhor**], the opener of every womb [**peter kol-rechem**] among the sons of Israel, among man and among beast — it is Mine."

## The Feast of Unleavened Bread Remembered (vv. 3–10)

**3** And Moses said to the people, "Remember [**zakhor**] this day, on which you went out from Egypt, from the house of slaves [**mibbet avadim**], for by strength of hand [**bechozeq yad**] the LORD brought you out from this place. And leavened bread [**chamets**] shall not be eaten.

**4** Today you are going out, in the month of Aviv [**bechodesh ha'Aviv**].

**5** And it shall be, when the LORD brings you to the land of the Canaanite and the Hittite and the Amorite and the Hivite and the Jebusite, which He swore to your fathers to give to you, a land flowing with milk and honey [**erets zavat chalav udevash**], then you shall keep [**ve'avadta**] this service [**avodah**] in this month.

**6** Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread [**matstsot**], and on the seventh day there shall be a feast [**chag**] to the LORD.

**7** Unleavened bread shall be eaten for the seven days, and leavened bread [**chamets**] shall not be seen with you, and leaven [**se'or**] shall not be seen with you in all your territory [**gevulekha**].

**8** And you shall tell [**vehiggadta**] your son on that day, saying, 'It is because of what the LORD did for me when I went out from Egypt.'

**9** And it shall be for you as a sign [**le'ot**] upon your hand and as a memorial [**ulezikkaron**] between your eyes [**bein einekha**], so that the law of the LORD [**torat YHWH**] may be in your mouth, for with a strong hand [**beyad chazaqah**] the LORD brought you out from Egypt.

**10** And you shall keep this statute [**chuqqah**] at its appointed time [**lemo'adah**], from year to year [**miyyamim yamimah**].

## The Law of the Firstborn (vv. 11–16)

**11** And it shall be, when the LORD brings you to the land of the Canaanite, as He swore to you and to your fathers, and gives it to you,

**12** then you shall set apart [**veha'avarta**] to the LORD every opener of the womb [**kol-peter rechem**], and every firstling [**peter**] of the offspring of a beast [**sheger behemah**] that belongs to you — the males shall be the LORD's.

**13** And every firstling of a donkey [**peter chamor**] you shall redeem [**tifdeh**] with a lamb [**seh**]; and if you do not redeem it, then you shall break its neck [**va'arafto**]. And every firstborn of man [**bekhor adam**] among your sons you shall redeem.

**14** And it shall be, when your son asks you tomorrow [**machar**], saying, 'What is this?' then you shall say to him, 'By strength of hand [**bechozeq yad**] the LORD brought us out from Egypt, from the house of slaves.

**15** And it was, when Pharaoh was stubborn [**hiqshah**] about sending us away, that the LORD killed every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of man to the firstborn of beast. Therefore I sacrifice [**zoveach**] to the LORD every opener of the womb [**kol-peter rechem**] that is male, and every firstborn of my sons I redeem.'

**16** And it shall be as a sign [**le'ot**] upon your hand and as frontlets [**totafot**] between your eyes, for by strength of hand [**bechozeq yad**] the LORD brought us out from Egypt."

## The Wilderness Route (vv. 17–18)

**17** And it was, when Pharaoh sent the people away, that God did not lead them by way of the land of the Philistines [**derekh erets Pelishtim**], though it was near [**qarov**], for God said, "Lest the people change their minds [**yinnachem**] when they see war, and return to Egypt."

**18** And God caused the people to go around by way of the wilderness [**derekh hammidbar**] of the Sea of Reeds [**Yam-Suf**]. And the sons of Israel went up armed [**chamushim**] from the land of Egypt.

## Joseph's Bones (vv. 19–20)

**19** And Moses took the bones of Joseph [**atsmot Yosef**] with him, for he had made the sons of Israel solemnly swear [**hashbea hishbia**], saying, "God will surely visit [**paqod yifqod**] you, and you shall bring up my bones from here with you."

**20** And they journeyed from Succoth and encamped at Etham, at the edge of the wilderness [**biqtseh hammidbar**].

## The Pillar of Cloud and Fire (vv. 21–22)

**21** And the LORD was going before them by day in a pillar of cloud [**be'ammud anan**] to lead them on the way, and by night in a pillar of fire [**be'ammud esh**] to give light to them, to go by day and by night.

**22** **He did not remove [**lo-yamish**] the pillar of cloud by day or the pillar of fire by night from before the people.**

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folios ~39r–39v (PDF pp. 75–76) contain the entirety of Exodus 13 in the standard three-column layout with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. The chapter begins on the first page (page 75, continuing from the end of chapter 12) and extends into the second page (page 76). A setumah (closed paragraph) marker is visible before 13:1, indicating a thematic sub-division within the larger Passover-departure unit that began at 12:1. The text of 13:1–2 opens with vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor — notably, unlike 12:1, Aaron is not addressed; this is a command to Moses alone. A setumah marker appears before v. 3, where Moses shifts from receiving the divine command to transmitting it to the people. Another setumah marker appears before v. 11, marking the transition from the unleavened bread legislation to the firstborn law. A petuchah (open paragraph) marker appears before v. 17, signaling the major shift from legislation to narrative — from the laws of Passover and firstborn to the account of the wilderness route. The masorah parva annotations are visible throughout, including notations on the term peter rechem (v. 2), on chamushim (v. 18), and on the doubled verbal form hashbea hishbia (v. 19). The cantillation on v. 21 places distinctive emphasis on be'ammud anan ("in a pillar of cloud") and be'ammud esh ("in a pillar of fire"), highlighting the theophanic significance of these terms. The final verse (v. 22) is marked with distinctive cantillation that signals the conclusion of the chapter's narrative unit.

The Codex Vaticanus pages 53–54 preserve the LXX text of Exodus 13. The text begins on page 53 (continuing from the end of chapter 12) with the chapter beginning partway through the page, and extends onto page 54. The Vaticanus text of chapter 13 closely follows the MT structure, though with several characteristic LXX features. At v. 2, the LXX renders hagiason moi pan prōtotokon prōtogenes dianoigon pasan mētran ("sanctify to me every firstborn, first-begotten, opening every womb"), adding prōtogenes ("first-begotten") as a clarifying expansion. At v. 5, the LXX lists the peoples in a slightly different order and adds the Gergesite to the MT's list of five peoples, making six. At v. 9, the LXX renders eis sēmeion epi tēs cheiros sou kai mnēmosunon pro ophthalmōn sou ("as a sign upon your hand and as a memorial before your eyes"). At v. 13, the LXX renders the donkey-redemption clause with pan prōtotokon onou allaxeis probatō ("every firstborn of a donkey you shall exchange for a sheep"), using allassō ("to exchange") rather than a cognate of padah ("to redeem"). At v. 18, the crucial word chamushim is rendered by the LXX as pemptē de genea ("in the fifth generation"), a strikingly different interpretation that reads chamushim as related to chamesh ("five") rather than as an armament term. At v. 19, the doubled oath formula is rendered as horkō gar hōrkisen ("for with an oath he made swear"), preserving the emphatic doubling of the Hebrew cognate accusative. At v. 21, the LXX reads ho de theos hēgeito autōn ("and God was leading them"), where the MT has vaYHWH holekh lifneyhem ("and the LORD was going before them") — the LXX substitutes ho theos ("God") for kurios ("the Lord"), a notable variation in this specific verse in the Vaticanus manuscript. Page 54 of the Vaticanus shows the transition from chapter 13 into chapter 14, with the chapter division marked by a paragraph break in the continuous text.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"Consecrate to Me every firstborn, the opener of every womb" (v. 2) — qaddesh li khol-bekhor peter kol-rechem:** Hebrew vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor qaddesh-li khol-bekhor peter kol-rechem bivney Yisra'el ba'adam uvabbehemah li hu. The imperative qaddesh (Piel of qadash, "to make holy, to set apart, to consecrate") demands that the firstborn be designated as belonging exclusively to God. The term bekhor ("firstborn") denotes the first male offspring, and this is further specified by the appositive phrase peter kol-rechem ("the opener of every womb"). The noun peter (from the root p-t-r, "to open, to release, to be first to emerge") literally means "the one who opens" — the first offspring to open the mother's womb. The noun rechem ("womb") specifies the anatomical reference. Together, peter rechem is the technical cultic term for the firstborn as defined by birth order from the mother (not the father — a man's secondborn son from a second wife would still be peter rechem of that mother). The claim li hu ("it is Mine") asserts divine ownership: every firstborn, human and animal, belongs to God by right of the Exodus — God spared Israel's firstborn when He struck Egypt's, and that act of deliverance establishes a permanent claim. LOGOS renders "consecrate" for qaddesh and "opener of every womb" for peter kol-rechem, preserving the Hebrew's anatomical specificity. The LXX renders hagiason moi pan prōtotokon prōtogenes dianoigon pasan mētran ("sanctify to me every firstborn, first-begotten, opening every womb"), adding the clarifying prōtogenes.

2. **"The house of slaves" (vv. 3, 14) — beit avadim:** Hebrew ki bechozeq yad hotsi YHWH etkhem mizzeh velo ye'akhel chamets. The phrase mibbet avadim ("from the house of slaves") is a fixed epithet for Egypt that recurs throughout Deuteronomy and the prophets (cf. Deut. 5:6; 6:12; 7:8; 8:14; Josh. 24:17; Judg. 6:8; Jer. 34:13; Mic. 6:4). The construct beit avadim is literally "house of slaves" — not "house of bondage" (which would soften the meaning) or "slave house" (which would change the grammatical relationship). Egypt is defined not by its geography, culture, or power but by its function: it is the place where Israel was enslaved. The phrase reduces all of Egypt's grandeur to a single characterization: a house full of slaves. LOGOS renders "house of slaves" to preserve the stark Hebrew construct. The LXX renders oikou douleias ("house of slavery/servitude"), using the abstract noun douleia rather than the concrete "slaves."

3. **"The month of Aviv" (v. 4) — bechodesh ha'Aviv:** Hebrew hayyom attem yots'im bechodesh ha'Aviv. The noun Aviv means "young ears of grain" — specifically, grain that has reached the stage of ripening where the ears are fresh, green, and beginning to fill (cf. Exod. 9:31, where barley is described as Aviv at the time of the seventh plague). The month of Aviv is thus the month when the barley crop is in its early ripening stage — roughly March/April in the agricultural calendar of the Levant. This month was later called Nisan (a Babylonian-derived name adopted during the exile; cf. Neh. 2:1; Esth. 3:7). The name Aviv anchors the Exodus in the agricultural calendar: Israel departs Egypt precisely when the land of Canaan is producing its first grain. The theological implication is that the land is already preparing its harvest for the people who are about to be led to it. LOGOS renders "Aviv" as a proper name, preserving the Hebrew term. The LXX renders en mēni tōn neōn ("in the month of new things/new crops"), interpreting Aviv as a description rather than a proper name.

4. **"You shall tell your son" (v. 8) — vehiggadta levinkha:** Hebrew vehiggadta levinkha bayyom hahu lemor ba'avur zeh asah YHWH li betse'ti miMitsrayim. The verb higgadta (Hiphil of nagad, "to declare, to tell, to announce") is stronger than simple saying (amar); it carries the sense of formal declaration or proclamation. This is the second of the four "child's questions" in the Torah (after 12:26–27; followed by Deut. 6:20–25 and the implied fourth child of 13:8 itself in rabbinic tradition). The answer given here is strikingly personal: ba'avur zeh asah YHWH li ("because of this the LORD did for me"). The first person singular li ("for me") — not lanu ("for us") — makes the Exodus a personal experience for each generation. Every parent is to speak as though they personally came out of Egypt. The demonstrative zeh ("this") points to the concrete ritual being performed (the eating of matstsot): "because of this [unleavened bread that you see before you], the LORD acted for me." The ritual is not merely commemorative but causally linked to the event. LOGOS renders "tell" for higgadta. The LXX renders kai anangeleis tō huiō sou ("and you shall announce to your son"), using anangellō, a formal declaration verb.

5. **"A sign upon your hand and a memorial between your eyes" (v. 9) / "A sign upon your hand and frontlets between your eyes" (v. 16) — ot/totafot:** Hebrew vehayah lekha le'ot al-yadekha ulezikkaron bein einekha (v. 9); vehayah le'ot al-yadkha uletotafot bein einekha (v. 16). Two parallel formulae appear in this chapter, with a key variation: v. 9 uses zikkaron ("memorial") while v. 16 uses totafot ("frontlets"). The term ot ("sign") upon the hand and something "between the eyes" became the basis for the later Jewish practice of tefillin (phylacteries) — small leather boxes containing Scripture passages strapped to the arm and forehead. Whether the original intent was literal or metaphorical is debated: (a) The metaphorical reading takes these as vivid figures of speech — the Torah's teaching should be as constantly present as something tied to your hand or placed before your eyes. Comparable idioms appear in Proverbs 3:3; 6:21; 7:3. (b) The literal reading, reflected in Second Temple and rabbinic practice, understands these verses as commanding the physical binding of Scripture passages on arm and head. The term totafot is obscure; it occurs only three times in the Hebrew Bible (here; Exod. 13:16; Deut. 6:8; 11:18), always in this formulaic context. Its etymology is uncertain — suggestions include derivation from a root meaning "to go around" (encircling the head), or from an Akkadian or Aramaic cognate meaning "band" or "binding." The traditional rendering "frontlets" captures the positional meaning (something worn on the front of the head) without committing to a specific etymology. LOGOS renders "sign upon your hand" and "memorial between your eyes" (v. 9) / "frontlets between your eyes" (v. 16), preserving both the parallel and the variation. The LXX renders sēmeion epi tēs cheiros sou kai mnēmosunon pro ophthalmōn sou (v. 9, "sign upon your hand and memorial before your eyes") and asaleton pro ophthalmōn sou (v. 16, "immovable thing before your eyes"), using the rare word asaleutos ("immovable, unshaken") to render totafot — suggesting something permanently fixed before the eyes.

6. **"You shall set apart to the LORD every opener of the womb" (v. 12) — veha'avarta kol-peter rechem laYHWH:** Hebrew veha'avarta kol-peter rechem laYHWH vekhol-peter sheger behemah asher yihyeh lekha hazzekharim laYHWH. The verb ha'avarta (Hiphil of 'avar, "to cause to pass over, to transfer, to set apart") literally means "you shall cause to pass over" — that is, transfer ownership to the LORD. The same verb 'avar ("to pass") that described God "passing through" Egypt (12:12, 23) is now applied to the transfer of firstborn to God. The wordplay is theologically deliberate: God passed through Egypt, and Israel now passes its firstborn over to God. The noun sheger ("offspring, issue of the womb") is a rare term (occurring only here, Deut. 7:13; 28:4, 18, 51) for the firstling of an animal — the first drop of the womb, as it were. LOGOS renders "set apart" for ha'avarta, capturing the transfer-of-ownership meaning. The LXX renders aphorieis pan dianoigon mētran ("you shall set apart every one opening a womb"), using aphorizō ("to set apart, to separate").

7. **"Every firstling of a donkey you shall redeem with a lamb" (v. 13) — peter chamor tifdeh veseh:** Hebrew vekhol-peter chamor tifdeh veseh ve'im-lo tifdeh va'arafto vekhol bekhor adam bevanekha tifdeh. The donkey (chamor) occupies a unique position in Israelite firstborn law: as an unclean animal, it cannot be sacrificed to the LORD, yet as a valuable domestic animal, its firstborn falls under the general peter rechem obligation. The solution is pidyon ("redemption") — substituting a clean animal (seh, "lamb") in its place. If not redeemed, the donkey must be killed by breaking its neck (arafto, from 'araf, "to break the neck"), a method that sheds no blood (since the donkey is unfit for sacrifice, its death cannot take sacrificial form). The same verb 'araf appears in the heifer ritual of Deuteronomy 21:4. The human firstborn is likewise redeemed (tifdeh, "you shall redeem") — a reference to the later institution of the pidyon haben ("redemption of the firstborn son") ceremony, where five shekels are paid to a priest (Num. 18:15–16). The root padah ("to redeem, to ransom") implies paying a price to release something from an obligation or claim. LOGOS renders "redeem" for tifdeh and "break its neck" for va'arafto. The LXX renders allaxeis probatō ("you shall exchange for a sheep") for the donkey's redemption, using allassō ("exchange") rather than a redemption term — a notable interpretive shift from ransom-payment to exchange.

8. **"When Pharaoh was stubborn about sending us away" (v. 15) — hiqshah Par'oh leshallechenu:** Hebrew vayhi ki-hiqshah Par'oh leshallechenu vayyaharog YHWH kol-bekhor be'erets Mitsrayim mibekhor adam ve'ad-bekhor behemah. The verb hiqshah (Hiphil of qashah, "to be hard, to be difficult") means "he made hard, he was stubborn, he stiffened." This is a different root from the chazaq ("to strengthen") and kaved ("to be heavy") verbs used for heart-hardening in chapters 4–11, though it appears in 7:3 (aqsheh et-lev Par'oh, "I will harden Pharaoh's heart," using the same root). Here in v. 15, the hardening is described retrospectively and without specifying the agent — Pharaoh simply "was stubborn" (hiqshah). The Hiphil form is intransitive here: Pharaoh stiffened himself. This retrospective summary compresses the entire ten-plague narrative into a single clause, presenting Pharaoh's resistance as the cause (ki) that triggered God's climactic act — the killing of every firstborn. The causal logic establishes the rationale for the firstborn law: because Pharaoh's stubbornness led to the death of Egypt's firstborn and the sparing of Israel's firstborn, every Israelite firstborn henceforth belongs to God. LOGOS renders "was stubborn" for hiqshah. The LXX renders hēnika de esklērunen Pharaō exaposteilai hēmas ("when Pharaoh hardened [himself] from sending us away"), using sklērunō ("to harden"), the same verb used throughout the LXX hardening passages.

9. **"Armed" (v. 18) — chamushim:** Hebrew vachamushim alu veney-Yisra'el me'erets Mitsrayim. The term chamushim is one of the most debated words in this chapter. Three main interpretations exist: (a) "armed, equipped for battle" — from a root ch-m-sh related to military equipment (cf. Josh. 1:14; 4:12; Judg. 7:11, where the same word appears in military contexts); (b) "in ranks of five" or "in orderly formation" — from chamesh ("five"), suggesting the people marched in groups of five or in a five-abreast formation; (c) "in the fifth generation" — the LXX reading (pemptē de genea, "in the fifth generation"), connecting chamushim to chamesh and interpreting it genealogically (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Levi/Kohath, Moses — five generations). The military interpretation has the strongest contextual support: the Israelites are described as going up (alu, a term often used for military ascent) from Egypt, and the preceding verse (v. 17) mentions the possibility of the people seeing "war" (milchamah). If they might encounter war, they would need to be armed. LOGOS renders "armed" following the dominant Hebrew interpretation and the immediate military context. The divergent LXX reading is noted for its significance.

10. **"Moses took the bones of Joseph" (v. 19) — vayyiqqach Mosheh et-atsmot Yosef immo:** Hebrew vayyiqqach Mosheh et-atsmot Yosef immo ki hashbea hishbia et-beney Yisra'el lemor paqod yifqod Elohim etkhem veha'alitem et-atsmotay mizzeh ittkhem. This verse fulfills the oath recorded in Genesis 50:25, where the dying Joseph says: "God will surely visit you, and you shall bring up my bones from here." The doubled verbal forms — hashbea hishbia ("he made solemnly swear," cognate accusative with Hiphil infinitive absolute + Hiphil perfect) and paqod yifqod ("will surely visit," Qal infinitive absolute + Qal imperfect) — are both emphatic constructions. The first (hashbea hishbia) intensifies the solemnity of the oath: Joseph did not merely ask but bound the sons of Israel with the most solemn possible adjuration. The second (paqod yifqod) intensifies the certainty of God's future action: God will without fail visit/attend to you. The verb paqad ("to visit, to attend to, to muster, to appoint") is a rich theological term — when God "visits," it can mean either blessing or judgment, depending on context. Here it means beneficent visitation: God will come to deliver. The carrying of Joseph's bones connects the Exodus to the patriarchal narratives and demonstrates that the departure from Egypt is not merely a political event but the fulfillment of a multi-generational covenant promise. LOGOS renders "solemnly swear" for hashbea hishbia and "surely visit" for paqod yifqod. The LXX renders horkō gar hōrkisen tous huious Israēl legōn episkopē episkesetai humas kurios ("for with an oath he made the sons of Israel swear, saying, 'With a visitation the Lord will visit you'"), preserving both doubled forms with cognate accusative constructions.

11. **"God did not lead them by way of the land of the Philistines" (v. 17) — velo-nacham Elohim derekh erets Pelishtim:** Hebrew vayhi beshallach Par'oh et-ha'am velo-nacham Elohim derekh erets Pelishtim ki qarov hu ki amar Elohim pen-yinnachem ha'am bir'otam milchamah veshavu Mitsraymah. A remarkable wordplay on the root n-ch-m appears in this verse: velo-nacham ("and He did not lead them") uses nacham in the sense of "to lead, to guide" (cf. Ps. 78:14; Isa. 63:14), while pen-yinnachem ("lest they change their minds") uses the Niphal of nacham in the sense of "to be sorry, to relent, to change one's mind." The same consonantal root serves both meanings: God did not lead (nacham) them by the short route, lest they reconsider (yinnachem). The "way of the land of the Philistines" was the coastal road (later known as the Via Maris), the shortest route from Egypt to Canaan — a well-traveled military highway with Egyptian fortifications. Despite being "near" (qarov), this route would expose the newly liberated slaves to Canaanite and Egyptian military forces. God's strategic reasoning is explicitly stated: the sight of war (milchamah) would cause the people to "return to Egypt" (veshavu Mitsraymah). The fear of military conflict would overpower the desire for freedom. This divine assessment is tragically validated in Numbers 14:1–4, when the people indeed wish to return to Egypt. LOGOS renders "lead" for nacham and "change their minds" for yinnachem. The LXX renders ouk hōdēgēsen autous ho theos hodon gēs Phulistiim ("God did not lead them by the road of the land of the Philistines"), using hodēgeō ("to guide on the way"), and mē pote metamelēsē tō laō ("lest the people regret/change their mind"), using metamelomai.

12. **"By way of the wilderness of the Sea of Reeds" (v. 18) — derekh hammidbar Yam-Suf:** Hebrew vayyassev Elohim et-ha'am derekh hammidbar Yam-Suf. The verb vayyassev (Hiphil of savav, "to turn, to go around, to cause to turn") indicates that God caused the people to take a roundabout route — literally, "He turned the people around" by way of the wilderness toward the Yam-Suf. The traditional rendering "Red Sea" for Yam-Suf is based on the LXX's Erythra Thalassa ("Red Sea"); however, the Hebrew suf means "reed, rush" (cf. Exod. 2:3, 5, where baby Moses is placed among the suf plants along the Nile). Yam-Suf is thus "Sea of Reeds" — most likely referring to one of the marshy lakes in the Isthmus of Suez region (possibly the Bitter Lakes or Lake Timsah), though the term is also applied more broadly to the Gulf of Aqaba (cf. 1 Kgs 9:26). LOGOS renders "Sea of Reeds" to reflect the Hebrew etymology. The LXX renders tēn erēmon eis tēn Erythran Thalassan ("the wilderness toward the Red Sea"), using the established Greek geographical term.

13. **"The pillar of cloud... the pillar of fire" (vv. 21–22) — ammud anan... ammud esh:** Hebrew vaYHWH holekh lifneyhem yomam be'ammud anan lanachotam hadderekh velaylah be'ammud esh leha'ir lahem lalekhet yomam valaylah. Lo-yamish ammud he'anan yomam ve'ammud ha'esh laylah lifney ha'am. The noun ammud ("pillar, column") denotes a vertical standing structure — here, a column of cloud by day and a column of fire by night. The two pillars serve complementary functions: the cloud pillar guides (lanachotam hadderekh, "to lead them on the way") by day, and the fire pillar illuminates (leha'ir lahem, "to give light to them") by night. Together they make continuous travel possible — yomam valaylah ("by day and by night"). The verb holekh ("going," Qal active participle of halakh) depicts the LORD as actively walking before the people — a strikingly anthropomorphic image. God is not merely sending guidance from a distance but personally marching at the head of the column. The closing statement lo-yamish ("He did not remove" or "it did not depart," from the root m-w-sh or m-y-sh, "to depart, to remove") affirms the constancy of the divine presence: the pillar never withdrew. The shift from YHWH as subject in v. 21 to the impersonal ammud as subject in v. 22 is deliberate: the LORD is identified with the pillar, yet the pillar also has its own persistent presence. The LXX renders ho de theos hēgeito autōn hēmeras men en stulō nephelēs deixai autois tēn hodon tēn de nukta en stulō puros ("and God was leading them by day in a pillar of cloud to show them the way, and by night in a pillar of fire"), notably reading ho theos ("God") where the MT reads YHWH. The LXX of v. 22 renders ouk exelipen ho stulos tēs nephelēs hēmeras kai ho stulos tou puros nuktos ("the pillar of cloud did not fail by day nor the pillar of fire by night"), using ekleipō ("to fail, to give out").

### Hardening Verb Tracking — Exodus 13

| Verse | Hebrew | Verb/Root | Form | Agent | LOGOS Rendering |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 13:15 | hiqshah Par'oh leshallechenu | qashah | Hiphil (intransitive) | Pharaoh (retrospective) | "Pharaoh was stubborn" |

*Note: This is the first appearance of the qashah root in a hardening context since 7:3 (where God announced "I will harden [aqsheh] Pharaoh's heart"). Here in 13:15, the verb appears in retrospective summary within Moses' instruction for the child's question. The Hiphil is used intransitively — Pharaoh "stiffened" without specifying what he stiffened (the object "his heart" is implied but absent). This is a narrative compression: the entire cycle of hardening from chapters 4–11 is reduced to a single verb. The absence of a divine agent in 13:15 is notable — in the pedagogical context of teaching children, the emphasis falls on Pharaoh's responsibility rather than on divine causation. The next hardening verb will appear in 14:4 (chizzaqti, "I will strengthen," Piel, divine agent), when God hardens Pharaoh's heart for the final pursuit to the Sea of Reeds.*

### Theological Note: The Logic of the Firstborn

Exodus 13 completes the theological argument begun in chapter 12 by establishing the permanent implications of the tenth plague. The chapter's structure moves from divine command (vv. 1–2: consecrate the firstborn) to liturgical instruction (vv. 3–10: the feast of unleavened bread) to legal application (vv. 11–16: the firstborn law) to narrative continuation (vv. 17–22: the wilderness route). This four-part structure mirrors the pattern of revelation-instruction-law-action that characterizes the Sinai legislation to come.

The theological center of the chapter is the logic of firstborn consecration, which rests on a principle of substitutionary ownership. In 12:29, the LORD struck every firstborn of Egypt. In 12:13, He passed over every firstborn of Israel. The firstborn of Israel were spared not by their own merit but by the blood of the Passover lamb. They were, in effect, purchased from death. The command of 13:2 — "Consecrate to Me every firstborn... it is Mine" — draws the legal conclusion from this act of deliverance: what God has spared, God now owns. The firstborn belongs to God because God is the one who allowed it to live. This is not arbitrary divine claim but covenantal consequence: the cost of Israel's deliverance from the tenth plague is the perpetual dedication of Israel's firstborn.

The chapter distinguishes three categories of firstborn and their respective dispositions: (a) Clean animals (sheep, goats, cattle) — sacrificed to the LORD (v. 12, "the males shall be the LORD's"; v. 15, "I sacrifice to the LORD every opener of the womb that is male"). (b) Unclean animals (donkeys) — redeemed by substitution of a lamb, or killed by neck-breaking if not redeemed (v. 13a). (c) Human firstborn sons — redeemed (v. 13b, "every firstborn of man among your sons you shall redeem"). The human firstborn cannot be sacrificed (a point that distinguishes Israelite religion from its Canaanite context, where child sacrifice was known), nor can he simply be dedicated to secular life. He must be redeemed — ransomed from the divine claim by a payment that acknowledges God's ownership while releasing the child to the human family. The institution of the Levites later serves as a corporate substitution for the firstborn of all Israel (Num. 3:12–13, 40–51): the tribe of Levi replaces the individual firstborn sons in God's service.

The two "sign" passages (vv. 9 and 16) frame the firstborn law and create a parallel with the blood-sign of 12:13. The blood on the doorposts was le'ot ("as a sign") of divine protection; now the unleavened bread (v. 9) and the firstborn consecration (v. 16) are le'ot ("as a sign") of divine ownership. The shift from ot al-habbattim ("a sign upon the houses," 12:13) to ot al-yadekha ("a sign upon your hand," 13:9, 16) internalizes the sign: it moves from the architecture of the house to the body of the person. The sign is no longer on the doorframe but on the hand and between the eyes — the instruments of action and perception. The entire person, not merely the dwelling, is marked as belonging to God.

The narrative section (vv. 17–22) introduces a theme that will dominate the wilderness narrative: God's strategic guidance of Israel's journey. The avoidance of the coastal road (derekh erets Pelishtim) in favor of the wilderness route (derekh hammidbar) is explained by God's assessment of the people's psychological fragility: they would "change their minds" (yinnachem) at the sight of war. This divine accommodation to human weakness is a recurring pattern in the Pentateuch — God does not lead Israel by the shortest path but by the path they can endure. The roundabout way is not a detour but a formation: the wilderness will become the space in which Israel is shaped from a crowd of liberated slaves into a covenanted people.

The carrying of Joseph's bones (v. 19) ties the Exodus to the patriarchal promise. Joseph's dying request (Gen. 50:25) was an act of faith in the promise God had made to Abraham (Gen. 15:13–14): Israel would be enslaved, but God would deliver them and bring them to the promised land. The bones are a witness — they testify that the present event was foreseen and promised generations ago. Moses' act of carrying the bones links the Exodus to the Abrahamic covenant and makes the departure from Egypt not a new beginning but a continuation of a story that began with the call of Abraham.

The chapter's closing image — the pillar of cloud by day and fire by night (vv. 21–22) — is the first appearance of what will become the defining symbol of God's presence in the wilderness. The pillar serves three functions: guidance (lanachotam hadderekh, "to lead them on the way"), illumination (leha'ir lahem, "to give light to them"), and constant presence (lo-yamish, "He did not remove it"). The dual form — cloud by day, fire by night — ensures uninterrupted divine accompaniment. The LORD who walked before them (holekh lifneyhem) is depicted as personally leading the march, a divine commander at the head of His people's column. The pillar will reappear at the Sea of Reeds (14:19–20, 24), at Sinai (Num. 12:5; Deut. 31:15), and throughout the wilderness journey (Num. 14:14), becoming the visible sign of the invisible God who dwells among His people.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 13 (pages 53–54) preserves the LXX version with several notable features. At v. 2, the LXX adds prōtogenes ("first-begotten") as a clarifying gloss on prōtotokon ("firstborn"), a double rendering that is characteristic of the LXX's tendency to explicate Hebrew technical terms. At v. 5, the LXX adds the Gergesites to the MT's list of five peoples (Canaanite, Hittite, Amorite, Hivite, Jebusite), making six — this expansion appears in several LXX manuscripts and reflects a harmonizing tendency with other people-lists that include seven nations (cf. Deut. 7:1). At v. 13, the LXX renders the donkey-redemption with allaxeis probatō ("you shall exchange for a sheep"), using the concept of exchange rather than redemption (padah) — a significant interpretive shift that may reflect a different understanding of the firstborn transaction. At v. 15, the LXX renders the hardening with esklērunen Pharaō ("Pharaoh hardened"), preserving the intransitive Hiphil sense of the MT. The most striking divergence occurs at v. 18, where the LXX renders chamushim as pemptē de genea ("in the fifth generation"), reading the consonantal root ch-m-sh as "five" rather than "armed" — this interpretation counts five generations from Abraham to the Exodus (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Levi, Moses or similar). The MT's military reading ("armed") has stronger contextual support (v. 17 mentions "war"), but the LXX's genealogical reading has its own internal logic and was known in rabbinic tradition. At v. 21, the Vaticanus text reads ho de theos ("and God") where the MT reads vaYHWH ("and the LORD") — a substitution of the generic divine name for the personal name, possibly reflecting scribal tendencies in the Vaticanus manuscript at this point. Throughout, the Vaticanus text maintains close structural fidelity to the MT while exhibiting the characteristic LXX features of lexical clarification and occasional theological reinterpretation.
